title: Ronald C Kessler: The role of epidemiology in clinical psychiatry
text:
i am an epidemiologist i'm not a
clinician
until not too many years ago i was not
terribly interested
in clinical questions most of my work
focused on estimating the prevalence of
mental disorders
risk factors for onset and course of
illness that could be used to target
interventions done by clinicians but
without me thinking a great deal about
what those interventions were or how
effective they were
but about a decade ago i got involved in
an epidemiological study
uh funded by uh dr insole here the in
the
us army in the united states studying
uh patterns of suicide
among people in the military and the
explicit goal of the study was not just
to do descriptive analysis
but to try to give guidance to
clinicians about what should be done
this is an enormous problem although the
suicide rate is going down in most parts
of the world
it's increasing in the united states
particularly among young people
right now there are about 20 suicides
per day
in the united states among veterans
military veterans
and the question is what do we do about
it well
when we started doing these
epidemiological studies we found
somewhat to my surprise that a
substantial percentage in many cases the
majority
of the people who died by suicide had
been in treatment for mental health
problems
um the vast vast majority of course had
a history of psychopathology
as discovered by psychological autopsy
studies after the fact but the majority
of these people
were known to the treatment system in
most cases they were not in treatment at
the time
of their death but they had been in
treatment and often cases were in
treatment
multiple times and failed so the
question is
is um is the treatment of mental
disorders that bad
that we uh that we can't prevent these
people from
dying who are in our hands and and as
you know
doubtlessly better than i do i came to
learn after looking into the literature
treatments of mental disorders are are
in fact
um surprisingly good compared to
treatments of diabetes and asthma and so
forth
but there is uh there are a couple
things that are distinct
about uh treatments of mental disorders
that have become the focus of my
clinical epidemiological studies over
the subsequent decade and i came to
realize
that an epidemiological perspective is
something that's missing from clinical
research in a way that's very important
um the problem is that there are many
effective treatments for common mental
disorders
uh if we break an arm there's only one
thing we can do you go to the
er and you get your arm set but if you
have depression
you can go to a minister or a social
worker or a licensed
mental health professional or a primary
care doctor who gives you
an antidepressant medication or you can
go to various kinds of complicated
psychotherapies or combinations or
nowadays
infusions ketamine and so forth
but none of those things is optimal for
all patients
there are a number of studies that show
that about if you take one of the
evidence-based
treatments and randomly assign it to a
patient for depression say about 30
percent
their episode will remit and we know
from the star d
study and other studies that have been
done subsequently if you take those
people who don't remit and take another
one at random and give it to them about
25 will remit and if you take a third
one at random and give it to another
maybe 15 to 20
will remit and if we can keep people in
treatment long enough
it might be that we get over 80 85
remission
uh the problem is that um
uh depressed people are depressed and
they give up on treatment
and we found in the military that uh the
typical pattern is they go into
treatment and they drop
out and then six months later they come
in for another kind of treatment and
they drop out
and a year later they come into
treatment and after two sessions they're
not doing well and they say the hell
with it and they kill themselves
um so we started thinking that really we
have this
if we had kept those people in treatment
for the fourth and fifth and sixth
treatment
in the vast majority of cases we would
have been able to do something with them
but we don't have that many chances we
have to figure out how to get the right
treatments to the right patients right
away
and we're not currently very good at

not much is known about how to match the

to the right medication to the right

how old were you when it started your

have you ever gotten treatment have you

what's the cumulative probability of

you have to go to five people five
people you have to go till till you get
the question is among the people who

of the people who are not helped the

to a third and a fourth and a fifth and

uh it turns out that the reason that
there's the discrepancy between the 50
who are helped and the 90
who could be helped is because people
quit
people give up and

there is a significant association
between

all else equal

that's that's the helpfulness of

if you get the first treatment

the percent of people who were helped

the percent of people who were helped 20

going through the whole course is

there's very little replication and so
forth

the what we need to do to move forward

and i'm going to talk about those four
today and describe to you several
projects i'm involved in that are doing

forward

so much of the work particularly the

a network meta-analysis doesn't help us
with this because we're looking at
the individual level for that particular

and so you can't do meta-analysis there
are other ways of doing
meta-synthesis of individual level data
but not what we have right now
and the third thing i'll say more about

much much bigger sample sizes the embark

for example you know 300 and some people
three arms two phases there's 80 people
in

and that's the case with many of these

that doesn't work because if you only

you're just going to be replicating a

we need much much bigger samples and the

my sense is that clinical trials are not
